title: unordered_set
description: unordered_set's DSA patterns: contains-duplicate in O(n), and the clever O(n) longest-consecutive-sequence trick.
objectives: Solve contains-duplicate in O(n) using unordered_set
Solve longest-consecutive-sequence in O(n) using a 'only start from sequence starts' trick
Understand why checking membership is unordered_set's core superpower
Distinguish this DSA usage from the STL container mechanics already covered

sections.text: 1. Recap: Membership Checks Are the Core Superpower

The earlier unordered_set topic covered insert(), count(), and duplicate handling. Its real DSA value is answering "have I seen this exact value before?" in O(1) — let's see two problems built entirely on that question.
One pass, O(n) time, O(n) space — dramatically better than the O(n²) nested-loop comparison, or even O(n log n) if sorting first. This is the simplest possible shape of 'have I seen this before?'

sections.title: The clever part: only starting from sequence starts
points: numSet.count(n - 1) checks: 'does a number just before n exist?' If yes, n is NOT the start of its sequence — skip it, some other number will properly count from the true start
For {1,2,3,4}: only 1 has no predecessor (0 isn't present), so counting only ever starts there, walking 1→2→3→4, length 4
Without this check, every number would redundantly re-walk its own sequence — that check is what keeps the total work O(n) instead of O(n²)

sections.title: Checking guest badges at the door vs finding the longest unbroken parking row
sections.text: Contains Duplicate is a bouncer with a quick-check list, flagging the instant a badge number repeats. Longest Consecutive Sequence is like scanning a random pile of parking-space numbers to find the longest unbroken run — but instead of walking every possible starting point redundantly, you're smart about it: only start counting from a number that has no neighbor just before it, since that's guaranteed to be the true beginning of its run.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Sorting first for Contains Duplicate when unordered_set gives O(n) directly, versus O(n log n) for a sort-based approach.
Forgetting the 'is this the start of a sequence' check in Longest Consecutive Sequence — without it, every number re-walks its sequence, degrading to O(n²).
Iterating the original vector for Longest Consecutive Sequence instead of the deduplicated set — duplicates in the input would otherwise cause redundant work.
Using set instead of unordered_set for these patterns — the O(log n) tree lookups aren't needed here; unordered_set's O(1) average lookups are the whole point.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How would you check if an array contains any duplicate value, in O(n) time?
questions.answer: Iterate the array, inserting each value into an unordered_set; if a value is already present (checked via count() before inserting), a duplicate exists.

questions.question: What is the key trick that makes the longest-consecutive-sequence solution O(n) instead of O(n²)?
questions.answer: Only starting to count a sequence's length from a number that has no predecessor (n-1) in the set — guaranteeing each sequence is only ever walked once, from its true start.

questions.question: Why is unordered_set preferred over set for these patterns?
questions.answer: Because these patterns only need fast membership checks, not sorted order — unordered_set's average O(1) lookups outperform set's O(log n) tree lookups for this purpose.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: unordered_set's core DSA value is answering 'have I seen this exact value before?' in O(1). Contains Duplicate uses this directly: insert values one at a time, flagging a duplicate the instant a value is already present, for O(n) overall. Longest Consecutive Sequence uses it more cleverly: put all values in a set, then for each value check whether its predecessor (n-1) exists — only numbers with no predecessor are treated as the start of a sequence, and from there the code walks forward (n+1, n+2, ...) counting the run length. That single check is what keeps total work O(n) instead of O(n²), since every sequence is walked exactly once, from its true beginning.

sections.title: Trick to Remember unordered_set (DSA)
sections.text: Contains Duplicate = a bouncer's quick-check list, flag the repeat instantly. Longest Consecutive = only start counting from a number with no left-neighbor — the true start of its run.
